data.title: When Optimism Is Priced In: Investment Opportunities In The AI Era
data.description:
Transcript

Kimberley Stafford: Hello, I'm Kim Stafford, and I'm here again with PIMCO Group CIO, Dan Ivascyn, to give you an inside look at some of the recent discussions taking place within PIMCO's Investment Committee or IC. Dan, thank you for joining us!

Daniel J. Ivascyn: Thanks, Kim.

Kimberley Stafford: You kicked off the year with some global travel, spending time with our investors outside of the US. In fact, you happened to be on the ground in Asia during the recent election headlines and volatility in Japanese bonds. What stood out to you about how investors are thinking about markets? What were the range of perspectives around asset allocation and risk preferences?

Daniel J. Ivascyn: This time of year, I do tend to hit the road and meet with clients around the globe. And at these various events, we do surveys as well. So we actually have some hard data in addition to a lot of investor conversations.

There were a few themes this year. One general optimism towards economic growth, excitement about the riskier areas of the financial markets. A lot of excitement about global equities. A lot of excitement about the client's respective local market equities, but still not a tremendous amount of interest in global bonds or global fixed income.

So again, a risk-seeking mindset, but also a mindset where there is increasing anxiety or concern about some of the global themes or global uncertainty, policy uncertainty. Geopolitics was another area of focus for investors. So I think the bottom line is that investors are still confident. They are perhaps less focused on the global opportunity set than we are at the moment. But I think that creates great opportunity.

Kimberley Stafford: You've said previously that it's always important to compare the macro outlook with what's already priced into markets. When you make that comparison, where do you see pockets of value and where do you see potential signs of complacency or risks?

Daniel J. Ivascyn: We are confident about global growth. We are confident about US growth, particularly during the first half of this year. But when you look at risk markets, a lot of this is priced in. Maybe more optimism than is warranted is priced in when you look at very, very tight credit spreads, particularly tight credit spreads in the riskier areas of the credit markets, then global equities are at elevated valuations.

You combine that with the optimism I talked about earlier in terms of various client surveys and discussions, which would suggest that a lot of the good news is priced in. Our job as active allocators across markets is to look at what's embedded and implied in pricing. And a great opportunity is when you see significant optimism in one market, pessimism in the other, that invites a relative value opportunity.

Over the last several weeks alone, we've had volatility in Japan, volatility in the UK associated again with politics, which then bleeds into France. You have China at a very different part of their growth cycle, where they're dealing with disinflationary forces, while we have elevated inflation in Australia, across Europe, and in the United States.

It's a unique environment, an environment where you can do these simple relative value comparisons, and it can uncover a lot of attractive opportunities.

And I think that's why we say that in high-quality fixed income, your starting yield is a really, really good assumption in terms of the floor, in terms of expected return. But today, with steeper yield curves, more localized volatility, more policy uncertainty, you can add onto that base case yield by making thoughtful asset allocation decisions along the way, especially if you maintain portfolio liquidity, which means flexibility to react to future uncertainty.

That's very, very different than the model or the choices that investors had a decade or so ago when yields were low or negative with very little volatility, you had to lock up your money, you had to buy some of the riskier investments to generate that high yield. We're in a very different environment.

So again, we do think this is an environment where investors should acknowledge that a lot of optimism is embedded in prices. There's reasons to be optimistic, but from an asset allocation perspective, we think you could generate similar or even higher base case yields with more resiliency, more liquidity, more future flexibility than some of the popular strategies that have done well on a historical basis but probably don't do as well going forward.

Kimberley Stafford: Investors have enjoyed a long AI-led stock market boom. Lately, we've seen volatility amid AI concerns, including industry disruption, capital spending growth, and borrowing needs. From your vantage point, how is AI investment changing markets and where do we see investment opportunities?

Daniel J. Ivascyn: AI is a very important, innovative, and disruptive technology. It will, over the near term, drive growth forward. But anytime you have a disruptive technology, there’s going to be some winners and losers, and even in a strong economy, even in an environment where overall equity prices continue to go higher, they're going to be losers. So you have to be absolutely careful about this disruption. In some sense, looking back at my career in the business, I remember early stages of the global financial crisis.

People knew something was going on; didn't quite know what was going on. And again, the outcome surprised many investors. Early days of COVID. And I remember an Asia trip around the same time where I returned to the States, and it felt like something major was going on. But again, it created a lot of uncertainty. I think AI's a similar type of situation for markets.

We're in the early stages. Things are moving very, very quickly. And anytime you have this type of environment, you have to be prepared for the resulting disruption. Again, what that means to PIMCO is up in quality.

Be careful about locking up your money if you're not getting paid sufficiently to do so. Be careful about leverage. Be careful about concentrations, which gets back to this attractiveness of global diversification.

Focus on sectors that are less prone to disruption; more granular risk, like asset-backed risk, hard assets back at your investments; conservatism in terms of underwriting - all these are very, very important themes on a go forward basis. And all of these themes can be exacerbated if you have some type of negative growth shock. I think the playbook has to be different than the playbook that investors have utilized over the last decade or so.

But I think that the good news again is where we started the conversation, is that there's this very, very attractive, high-quality global opportunity set. But I do think that these asset allocation decisions will be quite consequential for absolute and relative returns going forward.

Kimberley Stafford: Great. Well, thanks very much, Dan, and thanks to all of you for joining us. We'll see you next time.

data.title: From Rides To Robots: Uber's Path Through The AV And AI Era
data.description:
Introduction

I recently wrote on Uber Technologies (UBER) back in November 2025. The article was titled Go From Rides To Riches With Uber Technologies. I discussed within the article that it had become one of the biggest battleground stocks in public markets aside from perhaps Meta Platforms (META), in part because of its treatment of gig workers, which some argue borderlines on abuse of labor laws, but also because of disruption risk due to mass adoption of autonomous vehicles.

The "pie in the sky" bull case that management and Bill Ackman are adamant to put forward is that mass adoption of AVs will actually increase the total addressable market for Uber. Uber could emerge as the central aggregator in an AV ecosystem because it already controls a large share of global ride demand and operates a highly liquid two-sided marketplace. Demand aggregation will attract more supply, and the AV fleet operators like Waymo and Tesla (TSLA) that own the capital-intensive assets would be better served to prioritize maximum utilization to increase their ROI rather than try to build the technology themselves.

Operators integrating their vehicles into the Uber ecosystem would provide immediate access to millions of riders, reduce their customer acquisition costs and maximize utilization. Uber benefits from brand recognition, sophisticated routing capabilities, advanced pricing algorithms, regulatory relationships, and a capital-light business model. In addition, its customers tend to be habitual, and may not be inclined to switch apps. These are advantages Uber has built up over the course of a decade and a half now and would likely come at tremendous cost to the AV operator to try and displace them, and go fully integrated.

Uber would continue to take commissions on rides while eliminating most of the driver-related costs. Lower ride costs could drive increased usage, and Uber’s platform could capture incremental take rates from a variety of AV suppliers, essentially increasing its profitability.

The bear case can be summed up in one word: disintermediation. If a dominant AV provider such as Waymo or Tesla did manage to establish direct-to-consumer relationships, especially in major metropolitan areas Uber operates, Uber would lose control of its most profitable markets. It may be extremely difficult for the providers to do this, but it's not impossible. Providers could subsidize rides or develop their own apps, thereby capturing higher-margin urban demand in cities like Los Angeles and San Francisco, which is where Uber currently generates a significant portion of its profits.

The risks of disintermediation have become a lot louder over the past few months as Waymos and Tesla's AVs show greater and greater promise, which is why Uber stock has sold off 27% from its highs realized in mid-2025. The company's 2024 results, however, were nothing to sneer at and showed no signs of disruption. Management also stated 5 misconceptions the market has about AVs, which I will discuss herein and discuss why I believe the stock more than prices in these risks and has asymmetric upside potential

2024 YE Financial Results

As mentioned previously, fiscal 2025 results that were released in early February were nothing to sneer at. Revenue increased to ~$52 billion, up ~18% year-over-year, driven by growth across both the Mobility and Delivery segments. Gross Bookings (the total value of transactions on the platform) increased ~19% to $193 billion.

Uber’s core usage KPI, Trips, saw strong growth in both rides and mobility. For example, in Q4 2025 alone, total trips were up about 22% year-over-year, fuelled by growth in both the number of monthly active consumers and trip frequency.

The lesser-known crown jewel of Uber's business is the Uber One Loyalty program, as increased adoption is indicative that the flywheel is spinning faster. A consumer may initially open Uber for a ride hailing, but their payment credentials, rewards balance, and other membership benefits can be applied to food delivery, grocery, and other services that Uber provides. Uber One simply strengthens this feedback loop by offering recurring benefits to its members, such as fee reductions and perks that apply across both Mobility and Delivery.

These rewards and benefits have the potential to compound but only if the user continues transacting on the platform, which creates switching costs as leaving Uber would mean forfeiting the accumulated benefits, convenience, and familiarity. Management has stated cross-platform users spend about three times as much as single-service users and retain at ~35 % better rates than those who use only one service.

At 2024 year-end, Uber One had already grown ~60% year-over-year to around 36 million members, showing acceleration in customer loyalty. Uber One membership expanded dramatically in 2025, however, as it grew to 46 million at year-end, up 53% from 2024. Of the 200 million total users, this means at least one-quarter of users are using at least one of Uber's services.

Uber not only grew revenue but also improved operating leverage. Higher adjusted EBITDA and operating margins reflect this, as they were able to increase their higher margin revenue streams such as advertising and loyalty monetization (Uber One). In early 2025, the advertising unit reached an annualized revenue run rate of over $1.5 billion, reflecting ~60 % YoY growth, one of the fastest-expanding parts of Uber’s business, but has since surpassed $2 billion on an annualized basis. Uber’s ad growth isn’t just about increasing frequency, as it benefits from high intent ad spend from users as they are essentially deciding where to spend on their trip destination and are therefore most open to suggestions from the app.

Normalized EPS grew to $2.46/share which is up from $1.35/share the year prior (a 82% YoY increase). Normalization is after one‑time accounting effects such as valuation allowance releases on deferred tax assets, investment revaluations, and other non‑operational gains. Free cash flow per share rose to its highest level in its history at $4.06/share up from $3.20/share in 2024, a 27% increase.

The free cash flow number needs to be taken with a grain of salt as Uber collects insurance premiums to cover potential accidents involving drivers. The faster Uber grows its driver base and the number of rides, the faster it collects premiums. Uber sets aside these premiums in reserves to pay out future claims, but Uber reports these premiums as an operating cash flow on the cash flow statement, which can make free cash flow look higher than what is being derived from core operations. As of F2025 this amount was $2.7 billion, so about $1.26 of FCF per share or 28% of FCF, which is not insignificant.

Addressing the AV Misconceptions

Management frames the AV debate around several misconceptions:

  • that AV growth will be zero-sum;
  • that lessons from San Francisco can be extrapolated nationally;
  • that AV operators can independently achieve high utilization at scale;
  • that scaling in only a few dense cities will capture most industry profits;
  • and that AVs will soon be capable of performing nearly all trips.

AV Growth as a Zero Sum Game

Management’s core argument is that ridesharing growth is driven by supply, not demand. Each incremental unit of supply improves customer experience through lower arrival times (ETAs) and competitive pricing, which in turn stimulates greater demand. AVs will simply create an additional supply source that will expand the total addressable market rather than cannibalize human-driver trips.

In support of this view, Uber has pointed out that in markets where AVs operate on its network, such as Austin and Atlanta, overall trip growth has accelerated rather than declined. Even in San Francisco, where Uber does not yet offer AV trips directly in their app, management has noted that Uber trip growth improved in 2025 despite the increasing presence of AVs in the broader market.

This isn't to say that this dynamic can't change if AV cost per mile falls meaningfully below human-driven alternatives. In this scenario, AVs may not simply expand supply but may actually replace it - this being if vertically integrated operators achieve sufficient scale and cost advantages.

Can AV operators achieve high utilization independently?

Uber argues that building an AV is fundamentally different from operating a global mobility marketplace. Ride demand is highly volatile by hour, day, season, and special event. Management claims that a hybrid model maximizes asset utilization because human supply can step in during peak demand while AVs operate at a steady pace during base levels of demand.

In Austin and Atlanta, Uber estimates trips per vehicle per day (TpVD) are roughly 30% higher than in other major AV markets and more than double levels observed in Los Angeles. Additionally, average AV ETAs in those hybrid markets are estimated to be ~25% lower than in other AV deployments.

The counterpoint is that these utilization advantages may be a reflection of AVs not having mature enough fleets. AV fleets are likely scaling supply ahead of demand to reduce ETAs and building trust with the consumer, which depresses vehicle utilization. As routing algorithms improve, fleet concentration increases, and as geographic zones are optimized, vertically integrated operators may close the gap as far as utilization and ETAs.

Furthermore, centralized fleet control theoretically allows AV operators to optimize dispatch and positioning in ways that decentralized human networks cannot.

Although Uber does complete as much as 10 million trips per day, and Waymo only completes 450,000 per week, which is a small fraction of the trips Uber does, Waymo's robotaxi trips grew from roughly 200,000 in early 2025, which shows rapid adoption and deployment. Waymo projects reaching 1 million weekly trips by late 2026. Not only that, but Waymo robotaxis complete more trips per day than 99% of human drivers and therefore can realize much higher utilization, so AVs really don't need to be scaled at a rapid pace to become a greater portion of the ride-share market.

Is San Francisco representative of Other Markets

They are adamant San Francisco is not the same as other markets Uber operates in. San Francisco is among the most tech-savvy markets on the planet, has a dense urban layout, an affluent customer base, shorter average trip distances, a mild climate, and a supportive regulatory framework for technological innovation. In many ways it is the perfect market to showcase AV technology and presents the best testing ground for operators like Nuro, Tesla, Waymo, and Zoox.

Uber emphasizes that utilization even within California varies meaningfully. Management points to public data suggesting AV trips per vehicle in Los Angeles are materially lower than in San Francisco, despite lower pricing in LA. Management argues that this utilization gap could pressure the economics of AVs as companies expand beyond the most ideal showcase markets.

That isn't to say that there cannot be a domino effect and that once one city like San Francisco greenlights AV technology, others won't follow. Especially in cities like Phoenix, Austin, and Miami that are known for high congestion and expensive parking but already have meaningful rideshare penetration and regulatory openness. They may fast-track the regulatory process to reduce the cars and accidents on the road if it shows proof of concept in San Fransisco. Over time, cost improvements could make even lower-income or weather-challenged markets economically viable as well.

Will AVs only need a few large cities?

Management shows unwillingness to acknowledge the idea that AVs will only need to scale in large metropolitan areas to capture the overwhelming majority of the TAM. Uber notes that its top 20 U.S. cities represent roughly 30% of Gross Bookings and 25% of profits. The broader “long tail” of suburbs, smaller cities, and rural markets contributes a substantial and growing portion of earnings.

Even within top cities like New York City, Boston, and Chicago, regulatory pathways for AV commercialization remain complex and slow-moving. As a result, human-driven supply is likely to dominate much of the geographic footprint for years.

However, dense urban cores tend to exhibit the strongest network effects, cross-sell monetization (e.g., advertising and membership), and AV economic viability. If AV cost structures end up being materially better than human-driven, long-term profit potential could shift back toward the major metropolitan areas rather than the "long tail" markets.

Will AVs soon be able to perform all trips?

Management argues that AVs remain operationally limited, citing non-service in lower-income neneighborhoodsweather-related shutdowns or delays, and infrastructure ououtages,hich shows the limitations of the AV network. Hybrid networks have been shown to provide greater resilience to these events, so human drivers can fill the demand AVs cannot.

The counterpoint is that automation may ultimately deliver greater rereliability,articularly for late nights, low-supply zones, or markets where driver elasticity is limited. Not only ththat,ut if AVs achieve superior safety, consistency, and cost performance, consumer preference may shift toward strictly using AVs. In that case, owning the full AV stack from fleet management, charging, insurance, and remote operations could prove strategically more valuable than coordinating third-party supply with Uber or Lyft (LYFT).

What This All Means For Uber

Management’s thesis rests on three pillars, which include ridesharing being supply-driven, so AVs expand rather than cannibalize demand; hybrid networks maximizing utilization and reliability as a result of volatility in demand; and geographic and regulatory fragmentation slowing down full AV displacement.

I believe in the near term, these arguments are compelling. Hybrid deployment greatly improves asset efficiency and operational resilience. Until AV fleets mature, the ability to utilize human supply during peak demand and maintain consistent ETAs provides a very tangible marketplace advantage.

Once AV fleets mature, the long-term outlook for ridesharing is uncertain. If AV cost per mile declines materially, fleet concentration increases, and vertically integrated operators solve peak-demand positioning through software rather than labor elasticity, today’s marketplace could look very didifferent, and the advantages derived by the consumer from the hybrid model may all but diminish. Control over fleet management, charging infrastructure, insurance, remote operations, and financing may prove to be core value-enhancing levers rather than value-add services. In this scenario, vertically integrated operators could capture more of the long-term margin pool than aggregators such as Uber.

Compounding this is the emergence of agentic AI. In a future where consumers increasingly rely on AI to compare ride options, sophisticated LLMs could access multiple platforms, including Uber, Lyft, Waymo, and other AV providers, simultaneously and simply select the cheapest or fastest option. This scenario would reduce the importance of Uber’s app as the primary interface consumers interact with and undermine its aim to be the aggregator of demand.

Ultimately, the AV debate is less about whether autonomous vehicles will scale, as they likely will; it is more about where the economics are realized. The outcome hinges on three variables: cost per mile convergence with the human-operated model, speed of regulatory diffusion, and whether Uber’s network advantages remain defensible in a world that becomes increasingly more reliant on autonomous fleets and autonomous software.

Valuation

At approximately $68 per share, Uber carries a market capitalization of roughly $145 billion. On headline figures, the stock trades at around ~15x earnings or 25X on an EV/EBIT basis. This places Uber in line with the premium tier of marketplace and technology platforms like Visa (V), Mastercard (MA), Netflix (NFLX). This implies the market still expects sustained double-digit growth and continued margin expansion. I prefer to use earnings or EBIT than free cash flow to value Uber as those metrics are not inflated by insurance reserves, in fact insurance accruals are an expense so actually reduces earnings.

Although the valuation may not seem "screaming cheap" today, 2025 results confirm that Uber is well on track to meet management guidance set forth for 2030. When we assume a constructive but not heroic scenario where Uber grows revenue through at a mid-double digit pace (~18% annually), supported by mid double digit booking growth in mobility and delivery, advertising expansion, and incremental take-rate improvement. If operating margins are further supported by higher contribution from ads, greater operating leverage, and less intense promotional activity, free cash flow could plausibly grow at ~20% annually over the next five years.

In this event, operating and normalized free cash flow could reach $14 billion and $18 billion, respectively. If the company is still compounding at roughly the same rate by 2030 and assuming the exit multiple remains unchanged at 25x EBIT, the implied equity value would be $244 billion. From current levels, that represents approximately ~70% upside or a ~20% CAGR over the next 5 years. For for detailed explanations of my assumptions, see my previous article.

This is before you include Uber’s equity stake in companies like DiDi and Grab, which they have valued at $9.2 billion, and its freight business. Although the latter has been unprofitable, it received an implied valuation of $3.3 billion when it was considering doing an IPO in 2023.

However, this somewhat "pie in the sky" scenario depends on several variables not just coming to fruition but coming to fruition simultaneously. These variables include, sustained bookings growth, margin expansion through advertising and cross-selling, integration of AV deployment, and minimal disintermediation from agentic AI. Each of these risks introduces uncertainty in our risk-adjusted valuation framework bringing more nuance than management guidance would suggest.

Conclusion

Uber remains a lucrative near-term play in ride-hailing services, supported by strong consumer engagement and high-margin revenue channels like Uber One and advertising. Long-term upside, depends on the evolution of autonomous fleets, regulatory assessment on their deployment, and potential disintermediation from agentic AI. When AVs achieve lower costs through scale, improved reliability, and increased adoption, having control over fleet management and the full AV stack could shift this value toward vertically integrated operators which creates terminal value risk for Uber.

Ultimately, Uber’s success hinges on its ability to maintain its role as a demand aggregator while navigating a future increasingly defined by AV technology and AI-driven consumer choices. I do believe the AV/AI risks are unlikely to be a factor in the near-term, which theoretically should not have a major impact on its current valuation when looking at this through a DCF framework. It is not a foregone concl either that Uber cannot be a demand aggregator in the long-term as AVs reach scale, which therefore would mean greater upside. For these reasons I believe Uber shows asymmetric upside potential and rate this a Strong Buy.
